worth_it_explanation: Account structure decides whether reporting, ownership, and demand routing in SpringServe map to the way a publisher actually runs the business. A hierarchy that grew by accretion, with networks and supply partners added as needs came up, can blur which entity owns which inventory and which numbers belong to whom. This check reviews the entity setup a publisher already has rather than rebuilding it, so it costs reading time, and the finding points to a concrete cleanup the publisher can act on.
intro: SpringServe organizes a publisher's setup into entities such as networks, supply partners, and child accounts, and that structure is what reporting rolls up to and what demand routing keys off. This subcategory is about the entity hierarchy itself, how cleanly it reflects ownership and how the publisher reads its own numbers, rather than the pricing or demand-routing rules that ride on top of it. These checks confirm that the hierarchy is organized so reporting and ownership are clear, and that inactive or duplicate entities are identified rather than left to clutter the account.

audit_checks.title: The entity hierarchy does not reflect ownership or reporting lines clearly
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When networks, supply partners, and child accounts do not map to how the publisher actually owns and runs its inventory, reporting rolls up along the wrong lines and ownership of a given stream of revenue becomes hard to trace. The numbers are not wrong in themselves, but they answer the wrong question, which makes reconciliation and accountability slower across every other audit area.

educate.base:
SpringServe groups a publisher's setup into entities, with networks at the top, supply partners and child accounts beneath them, and inventory such as supply tags attached where it belongs. That hierarchy is more than an organizing convenience. Reporting rolls up along it, demand routing keys off it, and permissions are scoped to it, so the shape of the entity tree decides how cleanly a publisher can read its own numbers and trace who owns a given stream of revenue.

A hierarchy stays clear when it mirrors how the business is actually run. A broadcaster operating several channels or apps benefits from each one sitting under its own entity so revenue for that property reads on its own. A publisher reselling another seller's inventory benefits from that relationship sitting in its own supply-partner entity so the resold revenue does not blend into owned-and-operated revenue. When entities map to real ownership and operational lines, a report broken out by entity answers a question the publisher cares about without manual untangling.

The problem the check looks for is a hierarchy that grew by accretion rather than design. Entities added one at a time as new deals or channels came up, named inconsistently, or nested under whichever parent was convenient at the moment, leave a tree where the structure no longer tells you who owns what. Reporting then rolls up along lines that do not match the business, and answering a simple question, such as how much a single channel earned, takes a spreadsheet and a guess rather than a single report.

A few signs point to a hierarchy that no longer reflects ownership or reporting lines:
  • Revenue for a single channel, app, or property is split across entities that do not correspond to how the business defines that property
  • Resold or third-party supply blends into the same entity as owned-and-operated inventory, so the two cannot be reported apart
  • Entity names follow no consistent convention, so the tree has to be decoded rather than read
  • Child accounts or supply partners are nested under a parent for historical reasons that no longer hold
You may want to confirm that the entity hierarchy still mirrors how the publisher owns and runs its inventory. One approach is to list every entity with its parent and the inventory it carries, then check each one against a plain description of the business and flag the entities that no longer fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster running several channels, apps, or free ad-supported streaming television (FAST) channels, each property usually reads cleanest under its own entity so its revenue, fill, and demand routing report on their own. You may want to confirm that owned channels and any syndicated or resold channels sit in separate entities. Blending them makes it hard to tell owned-and-operated revenue from resold revenue.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running shoppable video or product-adjacent streaming alongside editorial video, the commercial inventory and the editorial inventory often belong under separate entities so each reads on its own. You may want to confirm that the higher-intent commercial video sits in its own entity rather than blending into a single catch-all that hides how that inventory performs.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for networks, supply partners, and child accounts is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the full entity hierarchy in SpringServe, listing each network, supply partner, and child account with its parent and the inventory it carries, then check whether the tree maps to how the publisher actually owns and runs its inventory.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the account or entity view and list every network, supply partner, and child account along with its parent entity.
For each entity, note the inventory attached to it, such as the supply tags or channels, and whether that inventory is owned-and-operated or resold from a third party.
Ask the publisher to describe in plain terms how the business is organized by property, channel, and ownership, and compare that description against the entity tree.
Record every entity whose placement, naming, or contents do not match the publisher's description of who owns what.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by entity, with revenue, impressions, and fill rate per entity.
Check whether a single property's revenue lands in one entity or is split across several, and whether owned and resold inventory can be read apart.
Note any case where the entity breakout does not answer a basic ownership question, such as how much a single channel earned, without manual recombination.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the entity hierarchy, each entity's parent and inventory, alongside the publisher's plain description of how the business is owned and run. A hierarchy where entities map cleanly to properties and ownership lines, so a per-entity report answers ownership questions without manual untangling, passes. A hierarchy where a single property is split across mismatched entities, where owned and resold inventory blend, or where naming and nesting no longer reflect the business is a fail recorded with the entities that do not fit.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the entity hierarchy with each entity's parent and inventory, and the publisher's description of ownership, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the SpringServe entity hierarchy for {{site_name}} does not map cleanly to how the business owns and runs its inventory, so reporting rolls up along lines that do not match the properties and ownership the publisher cares about. A single channel's revenue is split across mismatched entities, or owned-and-operated inventory blends with resold inventory, which makes tracing who owns a given stream of revenue slower than it should be. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each entity to a real property and ownership line, separating owned inventory from resold inventory into distinct entities, and applying a consistent naming convention so the tree reads rather than has to be decoded. After the cleanup, confirm that a per-entity report answers an ownership question without manual recombination.

audit_checks.title: Inactive or duplicate entities are left in the account rather than retired
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inactive networks, paused supply partners, and duplicate child accounts that linger in the account add noise to reporting, widen the surface that permissions and demand routing touch, and create ambiguity about which entity is the live one. The revenue effect is indirect, but the clutter slows reconciliation and raises the chance that traffic or a demand connection points at an entity nobody is watching.

educate.base:
Entities accumulate over the life of a SpringServe account. A supply partner relationship ends but the entity stays. A child account is created to test a setup and is never removed. A network is duplicated to stand up a near-copy and both versions remain. None of these break anything on their own, yet together they leave a tree where some entities are live and some are dead, and nothing on the surface says which is which.

The cost is mostly clarity and risk rather than direct revenue. Inactive entities still appear in pickers and reports, so a practitioner building a report or wiring a demand connection has to know which entity is the real one. Duplicate entities are worse. Two near-identical names invite traffic or a demand path to land on the wrong one, where it earns or fails under an entity nobody is monitoring. A wider set of live-looking entities also means a wider permission and routing surface to keep correct.

Retiring an entity is not the same as deleting its history. The audit question is whether inactive and duplicate entities are identified and handled deliberately, by pausing, archiving, or clearly labeling them so they are distinguishable from the live structure, rather than left to sit indefinitely. A publisher that reviews its entities on a cadence keeps the tree readable and keeps traffic and permissions pointed where they belong.

A few signs point to inactive or duplicate entities left in place:
  • Entities tied to ended supply partner relationships or retired channels still sit active in the hierarchy
  • Two or more entities carry near-identical names with no label saying which is current
  • Child accounts created for testing remain alongside the live accounts with no marker setting them apart
  • No record exists of when entities were last reviewed for whether they are still in use
You may want to confirm that inactive and duplicate entities are identified and handled rather than left to accumulate. One approach is to list every entity with its recent activity, flag the ones with no traffic or revenue over a defined window, and confirm whether each is genuinely retired or still needed before deciding to pause, archive, or relabel it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, channels and apps come and go, so entities tied to a discontinued free ad-supported streaming television (FAST) channel or a sunset app can linger after the property is gone. You may want to flag entities whose underlying property no longer runs and confirm each is retired or relabeled so the live channel lineup is the only thing the tree shows as active.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, seasonal campaigns and short-run shoppable video can spin up dedicated entities that outlive the campaign. You may want to flag entities created for a finished promotion and confirm they are retired or labeled as inactive, so the evergreen commercial inventory is not mixed in with dead campaign entities.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for pausing, archiving, or removing entities is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every entity in the account for recent activity, identify the ones with no traffic or revenue and the ones that duplicate another entity, and check whether each inactive or duplicate entity has been retired or labeled rather than left active.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe console
steps: List every network, supply partner, and child account, noting its status and any label marking it as active, paused, or archived.
Flag entities whose name nearly matches another entity's name, which points to a duplicate, and note whether any label says which is current.
Ask the publisher which entities tie to ended relationships, retired channels, or finished tests, and record whether those entities are still active in the tree.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by entity over a defined recent window, such as the last 90 days, with impressions and revenue per entity.
Flag every entity showing no traffic or no revenue across the window as a candidate for retirement.
Note whether each flagged entity is genuinely dormant or simply seasonal, and record which inactive entities remain active in the hierarchy with no label setting them apart.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every entity with its recent activity and any active, paused, or archived label, plus any near-duplicate names. An account where inactive and duplicate entities are identified and handled deliberately, by pausing, archiving, or labeling them so they read as separate from the live structure, passes. An account where dormant entities, ended-relationship entities, or duplicate-named entities sit active with no marker distinguishing them from the live tree is a fail recorded with the entities involved.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each entity with its recent activity and status label, and any duplicate names, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} leaves inactive or duplicate entities active in its SpringServe account rather than retiring them. Entities tied to ended supply partner relationships, retired channels, or finished tests still sit in the hierarchy, and near-duplicate names appear with no label saying which is current, which adds noise to reporting and widens the surface that traffic and permissions touch. {{voice_recommend}} listing every entity with its recent activity, flagging the ones with no traffic or revenue over a defined window, and pausing, archiving, or labeling each confirmed-inactive or duplicate entity so it reads as separate from the live structure. After the cleanup, set a review cadence so entities are checked for ongoing use rather than left to accumulate.
